title: Brain Teasers & Quant Estimation
subtitle: Fermi problems, mental math, and classic puzzle patterns
intro: Quant interviews often test how you break an impossible-seeming question into manageable pieces. Fermi estimation asks you to approximate an unknown quantity by multiplying sensible guesses for each factor — population × fraction × rate — and tracking units carefully.

You'll also meet classic brain teasers: conditional probability traps, symmetry arguments, expected-value shortcuts, and combinatorial counting under constraints. The goal is not memorizing answers but recognizing patterns — when to use complement, when symmetry gives 1/2, and when to decompose a problem into independent sub-estimates.

Brain teasers reward pattern recognition over brute force: symmetry cuts a problem in half, complements avoid messy casework, and Fermi estimates show structured thinking under time pressure. Green Book and Brainstellar-style puzzles often disguise a simple invariant—equal odds for two symmetric players, or an expected value that linearity resolves in one line. Treat these as fluency drills: state the trick aloud, then compute.

primer.heading: Pattern, not brute force
body: Brain teasers are rarely about heavy computation. They reward spotting the **invariant** — the symmetry, complement, or linearity that collapses the problem to a single line. State the trick out loud, then compute.
Four reflexes cover most quant puzzles. Learning to match the **phrasing** of a question to the right reflex is the real skill being tested.

visual.caption: The four reflexes
items: Two **interchangeable** outcomes → symmetry splits the probability.
Computing an **average count** → linearity of expectation.
No data but you need a number → **Fermi** decomposition.

primer.heading: The complement trick
body: When an event is a messy union of cases, compute its **complement** and subtract from 1. The phrase **'at least one'** is the giveaway — its opposite is the single clean case **'none'**.
This turns multi-case bookkeeping into one short product.

example.prompt: Roll two fair dice. What is P(at least one six)?
steps: Complement of 'at least one six' is 'no six on either die'.

visual.caption: The complement rule, the single most useful brain-teaser shortcut.

primer.heading: Symmetry arguments
body: If **swapping** two outcomes leaves the setup unchanged, those outcomes must have **equal probability**. Symmetry can hand you the answer with no arithmetic at all.
Watch for symmetry that is **broken** by a small asymmetry — like one player moving first. Then you only need to account for the single thing that breaks it.

example.prompt: Three fair coins. Compare P(exactly one head) and P(exactly one tail).
steps: Relabel heads ↔ tails: the experiment is identical.
So `P(exactly one head) = P(exactly one tail)` by symmetry.

visual.title: When symmetry breaks
visual.text: In 'first heads wins, you flip first,' order breaks the symmetry. Solve the self-referential equation `p = 1/2 + (1/4)p` to get `p = 2/3`.

primer.heading: Conditioning shrinks the sample space
body: A condition **shrinks the sample space**. `P(A | B)` restricts attention to the world where B happened: count favorable outcomes **inside B**, then divide by the size of B.
The classic trap is answering the **unconditional** probability instead. 'Given a face card, P(king)?' is `4/12 = 1/3`, not the unconditional `4/52`.

example.prompt: Standard deck. What is P(King | face card)?
steps: Condition on 'face card' → the sample space is the 12 face cards.

visual.caption: Conditioning re-normalizes within the event you were told occurred.

primer.heading: Linearity of expectation
body: **Linearity of expectation** says `E[X + Y] = E[X] + E[Y]` — **always**, even when X and Y are dependent. Define an **indicator** for each piece and add the probabilities; you never need the joint distribution.
It turns intimidating averages into a one-line sum, which is exactly why interviewers love it.

example.prompt: Roll a fair die twice and add the faces. What is E[sum]?

visual.caption: No independence required — linearity always holds.

primer.heading: Fermi estimation
body: With no data, **decompose** the unknown into factors you *can* guess, multiply them, and track **units** so they cancel. Aim for the right **order of magnitude** — within a factor of about ten.
Multiplying powers of ten just **adds exponents**, which keeps the whole thing mental.

example.prompt: Roughly how many cups of coffee are drunk in the U.S. per year?

visual.title: Order-of-magnitude rule
visual.text: `10ᵃ × 10ᵇ = 10ᵃ⁺ᵇ`. Round each factor to one significant figure, add the exponents, and you land within a factor of ten.

primerNarration.title: Find the trick first
primerNarration.caption: Brain teasers rarely need heavy computation. They reward spotting the invariant — a symmetry, a complement, or linearity — that collapses the problem to one line. State the trick aloud, then compute.
bullets: At least one → use the complement
An average → use linearity of expectation

primerNarration.title: Flip 'at least one'
primerNarration.caption: When a question says at least one, compute the opposite — the chance of none — and subtract from one. For two dice, no six on either is five-sixths squared, so at least one six is one minus that, eleven thirty-sixths.

primerNarration.title: Swap and see what changes
primerNarration.caption: If swapping two outcomes leaves the setup unchanged, they must be equally likely. For three coins, heads and tails are interchangeable, so exactly one head and exactly one tail share the same probability of three eighths.

primerNarration.title: Shrink the sample space
primerNarration.caption: New information shrinks the world to the cases consistent with it, so recount inside that smaller world. Given a face card, only twelve cards remain and four are kings, so the chance of a king is one third — not four fifty-seconds.

primerNarration.title: Add the averages
primerNarration.caption: The expected value of a sum is the sum of the expected values, even when the pieces are dependent. Two dice each average three and a half, so the sum averages seven — no joint distribution required.

primerNarration.title: Decompose, multiply, check units
primerNarration.caption: With no data, break the unknown into factors you can guess and multiply them, tracking units as you go. Aim for the right power of ten — for annual U.S. coffee that is hundreds of millions of people times a cup a day times a year, landing near a hundred billion cups.

questions.question: A Fermi estimate approximates an unknown quantity by:
options: breaking it into factors you can guess and multiplying them

explanations.A: Correct. A Fermi estimate decomposes the unknown into factors you can each approximate, then multiplies them — for example population × fraction × rate.
explanations.B: A single blind guess has no structure and huge error; decomposition is the whole point.
explanations.C: Exact census data isn't available for most estimates and defeats the purpose of a quick approximation.
explanations.D: Tracking units at each step is essential to catch inconsistent decompositions.

remediation.question: Fermi problems are valued in interviews because they test:

explanations.A: Correct. Interviewers use Fermi problems to see whether you can reason in structured steps when exact data is missing.
explanations.B: They specifically reward reasoning over memorized facts.
explanations.C: Speed on a calculator is not the skill being tested.
explanations.D: Exact knowledge is rarely available; structured estimation is the goal.

remediation.question: A good Fermi estimate tracks:

explanations.A: Correct. Carrying units (people, years, cups) through each factor catches errors and keeps the estimate coherent.
explanations.B: Only the final digit ignores the magnitude that matters most.
explanations.C: Tracking nothing invites unit mismatches and order-of-magnitude blunders.
explanations.D: Exact values aren't needed — consistent units and reasonable factors are.

questions.question: Roughly how many piano tuners work in a city of 5 million people? A reasonable first step is to estimate:
options: how many households have pianos that need tuning per year
the exact number from a phone book
the city's area in square miles only

explanations.A: Correct. Estimating annual demand for tunings (households × piano ownership × tunings per year) is the right first factor to build the estimate.
explanations.B: Counting names in a phone book defeats the purpose of an estimate and isn't reliably available.
explanations.C: Zero is obviously wrong — cities clearly employ some piano tuners.
explanations.D: City area alone does not drive how many pianos need tuning.

remediation.question: Order-of-magnitude reasoning aims to be correct within about:
options: a factor of 10 (one power of ten)

explanations.A: Correct. Order-of-magnitude reasoning aims to land within a factor of about 10 of the true value.
explanations.B: Being exact is not the goal of an order-of-magnitude estimate.
explanations.C: One percent accuracy is far stricter than estimation requires.
explanations.D: A factor of a trillion would be a wildly useless estimate.

remediation.question: Supply ≈ demand in steady state means tuners needed ≈ tunings per year divided by:
options: tunings one tuner can do per year

explanations.A: Correct. In steady state, tuners needed ≈ total tunings per year ÷ tunings one tuner performs per year.
explanations.B: Dividing by total population double-counts and ignores per-tuner capacity.
explanations.C: Piano weight is irrelevant to how many tuners are needed.
explanations.D: A random guess abandons the supply-equals-demand logic.

questions.question: Quick mental math: 15% of 240 is closest to:

explanations.C: 48 is 20% of 240 — too high by a third.
explanations.D: 12 is only 5% of 240.

remediation.question: 10% of 240 is 24. 5% is half of that, so 15% = 10% + 5% =

explanations.A: Correct. 24 + 12 = 36.
explanations.B: 24 is just the 10% piece; you still must add the 5%.
explanations.C: 48 doubles the 24 instead of adding half of it.
explanations.D: 30 would be 12.5% of 240, not 15%.

remediation.question: To find 15% quickly, a common trick is 10% plus:

explanations.A: Correct. 15% = 10% + 5%, and 5% is half of 10%.
explanations.B: Doubling 10% gives 30%, not 15%.
explanations.C: Adding zero leaves 10%.
explanations.D: 15% of 1% is a tiny number unrelated to the trick.

questions.question: Two fair dice are rolled. P(at least one six) equals 1 minus P(no six on either die). P(no six) = (5/6)², so P(at least one six) =

explanations.A: Correct. P(no six) = (5/6)² = 25/36, so P(at least one six) = 1 − 25/36 = 11/36.
explanations.B: 1/6 is the chance a single die shows a six, not the two-dice 'at least one' probability.
explanations.C: 1/36 is P(both dice show six), the wrong event.
explanations.D: 5/36 is not the complement result; the answer is 11/36.

remediation.question: Using the complement is often easier when the question says:

explanations.A: Correct. 'At least one' problems are usually easier via the complement: 1 − P(none).
explanations.B: 'Exactly zero always' is not the kind of phrasing that signals the complement trick.
explanations.C: The complement is exactly what helps here, so 'never use complement' is wrong.
explanations.D: A single-outcome event does not need the complement shortcut.

explanations.B: 25/36 is P(no six), the quantity you subtract, not the answer.
explanations.C: 1/36 is P(both sixes).

questions.question: Three fair coins are flipped. By symmetry, P(exactly one head) equals P(exactly one tail). P(exactly one head) =

explanations.A: Correct. There are C(3,1) = 3 sequences with exactly one head, each with probability (1/2)³ = 1/8, giving 3/8.
explanations.B: 1/8 is the probability of one specific sequence like HTT, not all three.
explanations.C: 1/2 overstates it; only 3 of 8 equally likely sequences have exactly one head.
explanations.D: 3/4 would be six favorable sequences, more than exist.

remediation.question: Exactly one head in three flips means choosing which flip is heads: C(3,1) = 3 ways, each with probability (1/2)³:

explanations.B: 1/8 counts only one of the three favorable sequences.
explanations.C: 6/8 doubles the correct count of sequences.

remediation.question: Swapping heads and tails leaves the problem unchanged. This is a:

explanations.A: Correct. Because swapping heads and tails leaves the setup unchanged, P(exactly one head) = P(exactly one tail) by symmetry.
explanations.B: A Fermi estimate is an order-of-magnitude approximation, not this exact symmetry.
explanations.C: Confidence intervals are an inference tool, unrelated here.
explanations.D: Nash equilibrium is a game-theory concept, not a coin-symmetry argument.

questions.question: You roll a fair die. E[X] = 3.5. If you roll it twice and add the results, E[sum] =

explanations.A: Correct. By linearity of expectation, E[sum] = E[X] + E[X] = 3.5 + 3.5 = 7.
explanations.B: 3.5 is the expectation of a single roll, not the sum of two.
explanations.C: 6 is not the doubled expectation; 2 × 3.5 = 7.
explanations.D: 12 is the maximum possible sum, not the average.

remediation.question: Expectation is linear: E[X + Y] = E[X] + E[Y] even if X and Y are:

explanations.A: Correct. Linearity of expectation holds even when variables are dependent — independence is not required.
explanations.B: It does not require the variables to be equal.
explanations.C: It applies to random variables, not just constants.
explanations.D: Expectations are defined for these variables, so 'never defined' is wrong.

remediation.question: E[2X] for one fair die equals 2 × 3.5 =

explanations.B: 3.5 is E[X], before scaling by 2.
explanations.C: 6 is not 2 × 3.5.
explanations.D: 2 ignores the expectation entirely.

questions.question: A standard deck: P(king | face card). There are 12 face cards and 4 kings among them, so the probability is:

explanations.A: Correct. Given a face card, the sample space is the 12 face cards, of which 4 are kings: 4/12 = 1/3.
explanations.B: 4/52 is the unconditional P(king), ignoring that we're told it's a face card.
explanations.C: 1/13 is also the unconditional king probability, not the conditional one.
explanations.D: 1/4 would be 3 kings among 12 or a miscount; there are 4 kings, giving 1/3.

remediation.question: Given a face card, the sample space shrinks to 12 cards. Kings among them:

explanations.A: Correct. Among the 12 face cards there are 4 kings (one per suit).
explanations.B: 52 is the full deck, not kings among face cards.
explanations.C: 13 is the number of cards in a suit, not the king count.
explanations.D: There are 4 kings, not 1.

remediation.question: P(king | face) = 4/12 =

explanations.A: Correct. 4/12 simplifies to 1/3.
explanations.B: 4/52 is the unconditional probability, not 4/12.
explanations.C: 1/13 is the unconditional king probability.
explanations.D: 1/2 would require 6 kings among 12 face cards.

questions.question: Estimate U.S. annual coffee consumption (cups). A reasonable per-person daily estimate might be ~1 cup, population ~330M, so annual cups ≈ 330M × 365 × 1. Order of magnitude:

explanations.A: Correct. 3.3×10⁸ people × 3.65×10² days × 1 cup ≈ 1.2×10¹¹, on the order of 10¹¹ (about 100 billion) cups.
explanations.B: 10³ (a thousand) is far too small for a nation's annual coffee consumption.
explanations.C: 10⁶ (a million) still vastly undercounts hundreds of millions of daily drinkers.
explanations.D: 10¹⁵ overshoots by about four orders of magnitude.

remediation.question: 330 million × 365 days ≈ 3.3×10⁸ × 3.65×10². With about 1 cup per person per day, the order of magnitude is:
options: 10¹¹ when including cups per day factor

explanations.A: Correct. 3.3×10⁸ × 3.65×10² ≈ 1.2×10¹¹, so with ~1 cup/day the total is on the order of 10¹¹.
explanations.B: 10² is absurdly small for this product.
explanations.C: 10⁰ (about 1) ignores the population entirely.
explanations.D: 10²⁰ is far larger than the factors support.

remediation.question: When multiplying estimates, exponents:

explanations.A: Correct. Multiplying powers of ten adds their exponents: 10ᵃ × 10ᵇ = 10ᵃ⁺ᵇ.
explanations.B: Exponents subtract when dividing, not multiplying.
explanations.C: Exponents do not stay zero when you multiply nonzero powers.
explanations.D: They combine by adding, not cancel.

questions.question: In a group of 23 people, the probability that at least two share a birthday is surprisingly high (~50%) because:
options: there are C(23,2) = 253 pairs to compare, not just 23 individuals

explanations.A: Correct. With 23 people there are C(23,2) = 253 distinct pairs, and each pair is a chance for a match — far more opportunities than 23 suggests.
explanations.B: Everyone sharing one birthday isn't assumed; the surprise comes from the number of pairs.
explanations.C: 365 is large relative to 23 people, yet the many pairs still drive the probability up.
explanations.D: Birthdays are treated as independent and uniform; the effect isn't from dependence.

remediation.question: The birthday paradox compares:

explanations.A: Correct. The probability accumulates over all pairs of people, which is why it grows so fast.
explanations.B: It is not limited to adjacent people; every pair counts.
explanations.C: A single person can't share a birthday with no one else.
explanations.D: It compares birthdays (day-level), not just months.

remediation.question: C(23,2) equals:

explanations.B: 23 is the number of people, not pairs.
explanations.C: 46 is 2 × 23, not the count of pairs.
explanations.D: 365 is the number of days, unrelated to the pair count.

questions.question: Monty Hall: you pick door 1; host opens a goat door (say 3); you should switch because your initial pick had probability 1/3 and the other closed door now has:

explanations.A: Correct. Your first pick stays at 1/3, so the host's reveal concentrates the remaining 2/3 on the other closed door — switch.
explanations.B: 1/3 is your original door's probability, which does not improve by staying.
explanations.C: 1/2 wrongly assumes the two remaining doors are equally likely after the reveal.
explanations.D: Probability 1 would mean a guaranteed win, which switching does not give.

remediation.question: Switching wins if your initial pick was wrong. Initial wrong probability is:

explanations.A: Correct. You pick the wrong door 2/3 of the time, and switching wins exactly in those cases.
explanations.B: 1/3 is the chance your initial pick was right, not wrong.
explanations.C: 1/2 ignores that there were three doors at the start.
explanations.D: 0 would mean you're never wrong initially, which is false.

remediation.question: The host always reveals a goat and never opens the prize door. This:
options: transfers probability to the remaining door

explanations.A: Correct. The host's informed reveal never touches the prize, shifting the 2/3 onto the other unopened door.
explanations.B: The doors aren't made equal; the reveal is informative, not random.
explanations.C: It changes plenty — that is why switching wins 2/3 of the time.
explanations.D: The reveal makes switching better, not worse.

questions.question: Alice and Bob alternately flip a fair coin; Alice goes first. The first heads wins. By symmetry, P(Alice wins) equals:

explanations.A: Correct. Let p = P(Alice wins). Then p = 1/2 + (1/4)p, so p = 2/3. Alice's first-turn advantage gives her the extra edge.
explanations.B: 1/2 ignores Alice's first-turn advantage.
explanations.C: 1/3 is Bob's win probability, not Alice's.
explanations.D: 3/4 overstates the first-mover edge.

remediation.question: Alice wins immediately if the first flip is heads. That probability is:

explanations.A: Correct. On her first flip, P(H) = 1/2.
explanations.B: 1/4 is P(both first flips are tails).
explanations.C: 1/3 is not the single-flip head probability.
explanations.D: 2/3 is Alice's total win probability, not just the first-flip win.

remediation.question: If both miss on their first tries (TT), the game restarts with Alice to flip. The probability of this restart is:

explanations.A: Correct. P(T on Alice) × P(T on Bob) = 1/2 × 1/2 = 1/4.
explanations.B: 1/2 is one fair flip being tails, not both players missing.
explanations.C: 1/3 has no basis here.
explanations.D: 3/4 is far too large for two consecutive tails.

questions.question: Estimate how many piano tunings occur per year in a city of 1 million people. Which decomposition is most sensible?
options: (Population) × (fraction with pianos) × (tunings per piano per year)
One tuning per person per year

explanations.A: Correct. Fermi estimation multiplies interpretable factors with consistent units.
explanations.B: Tunings per person per day is not a natural unit; most people own no piano.
explanations.C: Dividing population by tunings alone drops key factors like piano ownership.
explanations.D: One tuning per person per year ignores that most people have no piano.

remediation.question: A good Fermi estimate:
options: Breaks the problem into factors you can approximate

explanations.A: Correct. Decomposition and order-of-magnitude reasoning are the point.
explanations.B: Exact data defeats the purpose of a quick estimate.
explanations.C: Multiple factors capture structure; one number is too crude.
explanations.D: Unit checks catch inconsistent decompositions.

remediation.question: If 1 in 50 households has a piano and each piano is tuned once per year, tunings ≈:

explanations.A: Correct. Households with pianos × one tuning each year (assuming ~1 household per person factor absorbed in framing).
explanations.B: Multiplying by 50 overcounts by orders of magnitude.
explanations.C: Dividing by 365 introduces a daily rate without justification.
explanations.D: 50 tunings total ignores population scale entirely.

explanations.A: Correct. Fermi estimation multiplies a few reasonable factor estimates to approximate an unknown.
explanations.B: Exact census tables aren't needed and defeat the quick-estimate purpose.
explanations.C: Random digits have no structure or accuracy.
explanations.D: Multiplying nothing yields no estimate at all.

explanations.A: Correct. Order-of-magnitude accuracy means landing within roughly a factor of ten.
explanations.B: Being exact is not the aim of an estimate.
explanations.C: One unit is far too strict for order-of-magnitude work.
explanations.D: One trillionth precision is unattainable and unnecessary.

explanations.A: Correct. 10% of 200 is 20 and 5% is 10, so 15% = 30.
explanations.C: 15 would be 7.5% of 200.

placementQuestions.question: P(at least one six with two dice) equals 1 −

explanations.A: Correct. The complement of 'at least one six' is 'no six on either die,' which has probability (5/6)².
explanations.B: (1/6)² is P(both sixes), not P(no six).
explanations.C: 1/6 is one die's chance of a six, not the complement event.
explanations.D: 5/6 is one die's chance of no six; for two dice you square it.

placementQuestions.question: P(exactly one head in three fair flips) =

explanations.A: Correct. C(3,1) = 3 sequences each of probability 1/8 give 3/8.
explanations.B: 1/8 is one specific sequence, not all three.
explanations.C: 1/2 overcounts the favorable sequences.
explanations.D: 6/8 doubles the correct count of 3 sequences.

explanations.A: Correct. E[sum] = 3.5 + 3.5 = 7 by linearity of expectation.
explanations.B: 3.5 is the mean of one die, not two.
explanations.C: 6 is not 2 × 3.5.
explanations.D: 12 is the maximum sum, not the average.

explanations.A: Correct. 4 kings out of 12 face cards gives 4/12 = 1/3.
explanations.B: 4/52 is the unconditional king probability.
explanations.C: 1/13 is also unconditional, ignoring the face-card condition.
explanations.D: 1/4 miscounts; there are 4 kings among 12 face cards, giving 1/3.

placementQuestions.question: Monty Hall: after host reveals a goat, switching wins with probability:

explanations.A: Correct. Switching wins whenever your first pick was wrong, which happens 2/3 of the time.
explanations.B: 1/3 is the staying win probability.
explanations.C: 1/2 wrongly treats the remaining doors as equally likely.
explanations.D: Switching isn't a guaranteed win (probability 1).
